title: Work & School
shortSummary: Talk about your job, studies, and workplace courtesies in polite and formal Korean.
culturalNote: Korean workplaces and schools run on hierarchy and titles. You rarely call a senior colleague by name alone; instead you use their job title plus the honorific 님, as in 과장님 (gwajangnim, manager) or 선생님 (seonsaengnim, teacher). Seniority by age and rank shapes who speaks first, who pours drinks, and who pays. The phrase 수고하셨습니다 (sugohasyeotseumnida, you worked hard) is said at the end of the day or a task to acknowledge effort, and it is more about respect than literal praise. Students address teachers with great deference, and the relationship between 선배 (seonbae, senior) and 후배 (hubae, junior) carries lifelong social weight even after graduation. Because so much depends on rank, the formal 합니다 style is common in offices and presentations, while the polite -요 style suits friendly colleagues and everyday exchanges.

grammar.explanation: Alongside the polite -요 style, Korean has a formal 합니다 (hamnida) style used in offices, presentations, and announcements. A verb stem takes 습니다 after a consonant and ㅂ니다 after a vowel: 가다 (gada) becomes 갑니다 (gamnida), 먹다 (meokda) becomes 먹습니다 (meokseumnida).

examples.en: I am going to the meeting.

grammar.commonMistake: Mixing 합니다 and -요 in the same formal context, which sounds inconsistent.

grammar.explanation: At work and school you address seniors by title plus 님 (nim), never by bare name. 과장 (gwajang, manager) becomes 과장님, and 선생 (seonsaeng, teacher) becomes 선생님. Omitting 님 with a senior is disrespectful.
grammar.commonMistake: Calling a senior by their plain name; always add the title and 님.

dialogue.setup: Hana, a new employee, chats with a senior colleague, Mr Lee, after a meeting.

lines.speaker: 하나
lines.english: Manager Lee, what work do you do?

lines.speaker: 이 과장님
lines.english: I work in marketing. And you, Hana?

lines.speaker: 하나
lines.english: I'm a new employee. I'm still learning.

lines.speaker: 이 과장님
lines.english: Do you have another meeting today?

lines.speaker: 하나
lines.native: 네, 세 시에 회의가 있어요. 좀 바빠요.
lines.transliteration: ne, se sie hoeuiga isseoyo. jom bappayo.
lines.english: Yes, there's a meeting at three. I'm a bit busy.

lines.speaker: 이 과장님
lines.english: Keep up the good work. You can do it!

lines.speaker: 하나
lines.english: Thank you. You worked hard too, Manager.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'What work do you do?' in Korean?
quiz.explanation: 무슨 일 하세요? (museun il haseyo) asks about someone's job politely.

quiz.question: How should you address a manager named Park at work?
quiz.explanation: Seniors are addressed by title plus 님, so 박 과장님 (Manager Park) is correct.

quiz.question: 감사_____. (formal 'thank you')
quiz.explanation: 감사합니다 (gamsahamnida) is the formal 합니다-style 'thank you'.

quiz.question: When is 수고하셨습니다 typically said?
quiz.explanation: 수고하셨습니다 (sugohasyeotseumnida) thanks someone for their effort, often at day's end.

quiz.question: Arrange: 있어요 / 세 시에 / 회의가 (there's a meeting at three)
quiz.explanation: Time with 에, subject, then verb: 세 시에 회의가 있어요.
